Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, usually blocking out about three hours a day across the week. I'm a visual learner, so I'd organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams that I could reference. I'd usually study in the afternoons when my mind felt sharpest. Sometimes I'd rearrange things if I had a lot going on, but I always aimed to hit my 22 hours per week because I knew I needed that structure to keep up.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up. When assignments were due, I'd definitely cram in extra hours and shift my focus entirely to that task. But even with the variations, I tried to maintain a baseline of studying every day. I found that having some consistency helped me stay grounded even when stress picked up.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I needed to check assignment details or watch lecture videos. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline or new material, and that would prompt me to jump on. I also logged in just to see if there were any announcements I might have missed. It wasn't always exciting, but it was necessary to stay on track with what was happening in the course.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became kind of automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee. I'd just quickly scan for any updates or new content before starting my actual study session. It happened naturally because I realized I'd miss important stuff if I didn't. The routine helped me feel more in control of the course even when the material got challenging.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visual content. I also found the assignment rubrics helpful because they gave me a clear picture of what was expected. Anything with diagrams or visual explanations stuck with me way better than walls of text. Those resources felt like they were actually designed for how I learn.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums, honestly. I only posted a few times because it felt like busy work rather than something that helped me understand the material. I also didn't engage much with supplementary readings unless they were directly tied to an assignment. Time was limited, so I prioritized what would actually show up on exams or in my grades.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exam prep. If I walked away feeling like I understood something better or could apply it, that was valuable. Visual resources that I could reference later also felt worthwhile. But if something seemed like filler or wasn't clearly connected to my success, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no pressure attached to them. I'd sometimes look at them if I had extra time, but they were always the first thing to go when I got busy. Required activities had a sense of urgency that made me prioritize them. I think I would've engaged more with optional stuff if the course load had been lighter.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then work through them on my tablet where I could annotate and highlight. I also made handwritten notes with diagrams because that's how information really sticks for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just me and my visual notes.
Q10: There were many times when I'd be at my desk for hours working through problems or reviewing my hand-drawn diagrams, and none of that would show up in the system. I'd watch a video once online, then replay it from my downloads multiple times. The LMS only saw that initial click, not the hours I spent actually processing the material afterward.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once to download it, then study it offline repeatedly. It was more convenient and I could study without worrying about internet issues. So my actual engagement with that material was way higher than what the LMS recorded from that single access.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really tough. I was surprised by how difficult the exam content was compared to what I thought I understood from the lectures. The stress was real, but I tried to use the mindfulness techniques I'd learned to stay calm and push through. Even when I felt overwhelmed, I kept showing up and doing the work.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had multiple assignments due at once, my LMS usage became more scattered and less focused. I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by everything I needed to do, and sometimes just close it and take a break. The workload made it hard to engage deeply with any single resource. I had to manage my energy carefully to avoid burning out.
Q14: There were times when I'd have videos playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about other assignments or just feeling mentally drained. I'd technically be 'watching' the content but not really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt unproductive, and I'd usually have to go back and rewatch things later when I was more focused.
Q15: I postponed some of the heavier readings because they felt intimidating and I wasn't sure where to start. Even knowing they were important, I'd tell myself I'd get to them after finishing 'easier' tasks first. Sometimes that worked out, but other times I ran out of time. It's something I'm working on because I know avoidance doesn't help in the long run.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd be logging in multiple times a day, double-checking requirements, and making sure I hadn't missed anything. Without deadlines, I probably would've spread my effort more evenly, but they created this urgency that drove my engagement.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it definitely influenced how I approached the next one. Positive feedback motivated me to keep doing what worked, while constructive criticism made me revisit certain resources. I appreciated when feedback was specific because it gave me direction. It made me feel like my effort was being seen and that I could actually improve.
Q18: The course structure was pretty straightforward, which helped me plan my time. Having clear modules and deadlines made it easier to break things down week by week. But sometimes the pacing felt off, with too much content crammed into certain weeks. Overall though, the structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed.
Q19: The visual materials and video content made engagement easier for me since that matches how I learn best. However, the heavy reliance on text-based readings made some parts harder to get through. I also wished there were more interactive elements or practice problems with visual feedback. When design matched my learning style, I thrived; when it didn't, I struggled.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to understand the material but just can't get it to click. They might be watching videos repeatedly, reading resources over and over, but still feeling lost when it comes to actually producing work. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism making them hesitant to submit. High activity doesn't always mean high comprehension.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of my learning offline, so my LMS activity was lower than my actual study time. If someone's efficient with their online time and does deep work offline, their performance might not match their login stats. The system doesn't capture everything that contributes to understanding and success.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active and what resources you access. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just clicking through. They also miss all the offline work, the thinking, the note-taking, and the real cognitive effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and apply the material. It's not just about logging in or watching videos—it's about wrestling with concepts until they make sense. For me, engagement looks like creating visual notes, asking myself questions, and connecting new information to what I already know. It's an active process, not a passive one.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew through this course despite the final grade. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices I used to manage stress or how I bounced back after disappointing exam results. My resilience, my offline study habits, my visual learning strategies—none of that shows up in the data. I worked hard even when the numbers don't reflect it.
